title: Noctua
shortDescription: A nocturnal identity system for a Brussels late-night listening bar, built on shadow, brass and a single recurring mark.
challenge: Noctua needed an identity that could survive both a matchbook and a projection wall, legible whispered across a dim room, not just on a screen.
approach: We built the system around a single owl-eye mark, a restrained brass-on-black palette, and a type pairing sharp enough to hold up at any scale. The wordmark bends slightly at the O, a quiet nod to the venue's curved bar.
result: A system stress-tested across signage, glassware, playlists and a monthly print zine before a single pixel was signed off, cohesive from a coaster to a projection wall.

title: Ferra Studio
shortDescription: An editorial site for an architecture studio, built around raw concrete photography and a slow, deliberate scroll pace.
challenge: Ferra needed a site that behaved like their buildings: quiet, load-bearing, unhurried, without becoming static or forgettable.
approach: We leaned on oversized material photography and long-form project narratives rather than grids of thumbnails, with every transition tuned to a deliberately slow pace. Nothing on this site moves faster than a visitor can read a caption.
result: A CMS structured so the studio's own team can publish new work without touching code, and a site that reads like their buildings feel.

title: Kinet
shortDescription: Design system + front-end architecture for a motion-capture SaaS platform used by animation studios across Europe.
challenge: Kinet had a powerful motion-capture engine and an interface that actively fought its own users, powerful, but nearly unusable.
approach: We rebuilt the design system from primitives up: a typed component library, a token pipeline shared between Figma and code, and a rendering layer optimized to keep 3D preview panels above 50fps even on mid-range laptops.
result: The visual system and the codebase designed as one artifact rather than handed off between disciplines, the project that best represents the VISULOX hybrid.

title: Monolith Records
shortDescription: Tour campaign visuals and social system for an underground label's first European headline run.
challenge: A six-city tour needed one visual language flexible enough to be recombined city by city without losing its identity.
approach: Built entirely in high-contrast monochrome around a single recurring crack motif: torn posters, glitched type, a look that photographs well on a phone screen from the back of a venue.
result: A modular kit, posters, story templates, merch, a countdown microsite, the label's own team could recombine without losing the system's spine.

title: Terra Nomad
shortDescription: A self-initiated editorial series shot across the North Sea coastline, grain, salt, and long horizons.
challenge: A self-initiated series with no brief, the only question was how far expired film's imperfections could be pushed before they stopped being happy accidents.
approach: Shot entirely on expired film, leaning into the medium's flaws rather than correcting them in post, light leaks, grain clumping and the occasional scratch treated as part of the composition.
result: A 40-roll archive, with a selection of 18 frames later exhibited at a small gallery show in Ghent.

title: Aether
shortDescription: A cursor-reactive WebGL lookbook for an experimental fashion label's SS25 collection drop.
challenge: An experimental fashion drop needed a lookbook that felt as considered as the collection, without heavy WebGL effects making mobile feel like an afterthought.
approach: Built as a single-page, single-purpose experience: sixteen looks, one continuous scroll, and a displacement shader that responds to cursor velocity rather than position. The faster you move, the more the fabric seems to ripple.
result: Heavy effects reserved for desktop; mobile swaps the shader for a lighter, still-cinematic mask-reveal sequence, so neither version feels like a compromise.

title: Vantage Capital
shortDescription: A full rebrand for a fintech challenger, from wordmark to statement templates to the pitch deck that closed their Series A.
challenge: Vantage needed to look credible to institutional investors without losing the edge that made them interesting in the first place.
approach: We rebuilt their mark as a monoline chevron system, paired with a restrained serif for long-form trust documents and a grotesk for product UI.
result: The system extended into their actual banking app and the deck their founders used to close their Series A.

title: Echo Chamber
shortDescription: A browser-based, audio-reactive visualizer built for an independent electronic artist's album launch.
challenge: An independent artist needed a launch moment for their album that would spread on its own, on a browser-toy budget.
approach: Built a tool that analyzes an uploaded track in real time via the Web Audio API and drives a full-screen particle field from its frequency data, no two plays render quite the same.
result: Shipped as a free browser toy timed to the album release, and later adapted into a live visual set for two festival performances.
